enrichment.summary: Kyle responds to a viewer request comparing high school and college across several dimensions: homework load, social dynamics, clique behavior, maturity, and ease of meeting girls. He shares personal anecdotes about being socially disengaged in high school, observing his brother's social anxiety, and his own limited participation in college.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: High school had more homework than college; you can get away with less work in college
People are generally more mature in college than high school, though Kyle himself was about the same
College friendships form around genuine similarity; high school friendships are based on proximity and longevity
Easier to meet girls in high school because the environment is more structured and intimate
High school people are more fake and trend-driven; college is when people discover who they really are
Social aggression and intimidation are more common in high school than college
Only go to college if you can use the degree for something; don't avoid it just because of workload

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: High school was more time consuming than college because he had more homework in high school.

claims.subject: Michael Ferai
claims.claim: Michael Ferai, a history of Central America professor, assigned so much homework that Kyle got kicked out of his class for not doing it all.

claims.subject: high school social dynamics
claims.claim: In high school, people gravitate towards acting like who they think they're supposed to be, often being fake or following trends like being a hipster or granola hippie.

claims.subject: college social dynamics
claims.claim: In college, people realize who they really are and change, often moving away from phases like being a stoner or partyer.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle didn't participate much in high school, didn't join any cliques, and mostly went home after school.

claims.subject: Kyle's brother
claims.claim: Kyle's brother was very plugged into high school social life, cared about appearances, and got embarrassed when Kyle acted weird in front of his friends.

claims.subject: high school aggression
claims.claim: In high school, some kids act aggressively to intimidate others, but this behavior is less common in college.

claims.subject: college fights
claims.claim: Kyle saw one fight in college, which was a party fight, compared to arranged fights he saw in high school.

claims.subject: social maturity
claims.claim: People are generally more mature in college than in high school, though Kyle felt he was about the same in both.

claims.subject: high school vs. junior high
claims.claim: There's as much difference between junior high and high school as between high school and college, with cliques being smaller in junior high.

claims.subject: meeting girls
claims.claim: Girls are easier to meet in high school because of structured environments like sports teams and parties, whereas college is less organized.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle didn't participate in college clubs except for joining the bike team briefly in his last semester.

claims.subject: social trends
claims.claim: From junior high to college, the social world expands, and people gravitate towards those more similar to them rather than just longtime friends.

claims.subject: college workload
claims.claim: The workload in college depends on the school, but it's possible to get away with not doing much homework and still pass.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Kyle got kicked out of school for not doing a required paper and never got his degree.
